schools.summary: The transition to the Peraean ministry on Christ's last journey to Jerusalem; much of Luke and John 7-11 intervenes (Robertson, Lightfoot); the crowds resort to him and he teaches as was his constant practice.
The crowds come to be taught, the Pharisees to tempt (Bede); Christ enters Judaea, where his Passion must be, to do good to the multitudes (Theophylact).
Henry: Christ the itinerant preacher teaches even those already taught again; Moses' permission of divorce was for hardness of heart, not to be used.

themes: as he was wont, he taught

schools.summary: The Pharisees ask not to learn but to ensnare (Robertson) — a question with a precipice on either side (Theophylact); perhaps hoping to entangle Christ with Herod as the Baptist was.
Henry: a good question if humbly asked, but put to tempt — ministers must guard against being ensnared under pretence of being consulted.

themes: is it lawful to put away his wife

schools.summary: Christ's prudent counter-question turns them to Moses (Deuteronomy 24:1), drawing the ground of his answer from their own mouths, before going back to the creation ideal (Genesis 1:27).

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the seed growing secretly (Mark 4:27), belongs elsewhere, not this verse.

schools.summary: The Pharisees cite Moses' 'bill of divorcement' (Deuteronomy 24:1), reflecting the lax Hillel view of easy divorce; Christ takes the stricter Shammai view, expounding marriage's purpose (Robertson).
Gill: Christ interprets Moses' permission as a concession 'for the hardness of your heart' — no honour but a confession of sin; to claim it is to admit one's own hardness (Henry).
Augustine: the written bill was a delay to deter the hasty and give the wise room to reconcile; only an unmeltable hardness proceeded to divorce (under v.1).
Christ appeals from Moses' permission back to the creation ordinance — God made one man and one woman (Genesis 1:27), condemning polygamy and divorce at pleasure (Wesley).
Chrysostom: had God willed divorce and remarriage he would have created several women; 'male and female' (Bede) binds them by the tie of one wife.

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on the great tribulation and Antichrist, is an off-topic catechetical aside, not on this verse.

themes: from the beginning of the creation

schools.summary: The foundational text (Genesis 2:24): a man leaves father and mother to cleave to his wife — marking both the nearness and permanence of the bond, supreme over all natural ties (Henry).
Chrysostom: 'he shall cleave' shows the impossibility of severing marriage — an inseparable union.
'No more twain, but one flesh' (Genesis 2:24) — the most intimate, sacred union, ground of the prohibition (Henry); Paul applies it to Christ and the Church (Ephesians 5:31).
Chrysostom/Bede: framed of one root they join into one body, figuring the inseparable union of Christ and his Church.
The climax: since God himself joins husband and wife into one flesh, man must not put them asunder (Henry) — divorce a presumptuous undoing of God's own work.
Chrysostom: an awful argument — if those God joined are not to be separated, much more the Church from Christ; Bede: man cannot separate them, but God alone.
A detail peculiar to Mark (Ellicott): the disciples ask again privately 'in the house' — the place of fuller instruction apart from crowd and Pharisees (Henry).
The disciples were not fully satisfied and question again (Theophylact); repetition of the Word breeds not weariness but hunger (Gloss).
Divorce-and-remarriage is adultery 'against her' — wronging the first wife by breaking the one-flesh bond (Henry); Mark omits the Matthean exception, though the principle is implied (Robertson).

themes: put away his wife and marry another

schools.summary: Peculiar to Mark and fitting his Roman readers: the wife who divorces her husband and remarries also commits adultery (Herodias, Salome) — the bond binds both sexes equally (Ellicott, Robertson).

themes: if a woman put away her husband

schools.summary: The children brought in great faith that Christ's touch would bless them (Theophylact); the disciples rebuke from a worldly pride Christ would tread underfoot (Chrysostom).
'Of such is the kingdom' — childlike innocence, simplicity, and teachable faith that receives without contradicting (Bede, Origen); ministers must not forbid the lowly brought to Christ.
Henry: the disciples thought children useless and beneath Christ's dignity; he was displeased that they would keep any from him.

Mark alone records Christ's strong indignation ('moved with indignation') at the disciples' rebuke; he welcomes the children — to hinder them is a crime (Robertson); 'of such is the kingdom' claims it for the childlike.

The kingdom is received only 'as a little child' — with simplicity, humility, faith, and trusting obedience, disclaiming all self-worthiness (Barnes, Wesley); the condition is absolute (no entrance otherwise), rebuking the disciples' dispute about greatness (Ellicott).

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the boat and teaching, belongs elsewhere, not this verse.

themes: receive the kingdom as a little child

schools.summary: Christ folds each child in his arms and blesses them (mentioned by Mark alone) — a rebuke to the disciples and a model for parents to bring children to Christ; Barnes/Clarke draw the dedication/infant-baptism application.
The embrace figures Christ reconciling his fallen creation to himself; the lowly in spirit are worthy of his blessing (Pseudo-Chrysostom, Bede).

themes: took them up in his arms

schools.summary: The ruler eagerly runs and kneels (details only in Mark) with a serious question about eternal life — every outward mark of earnestness and reverence (Robertson, Henry).
Bede: his question and confession are sincere, not tempting; yet Theophylact marks the love of money beneath, which would cause his sorrow.
Henry: a hopeful approach — running (humility), kneeling (veneration), asking the weightiest question; 'some hope when people enquire what they shall do to get to heaven.'
'Why callest thou me good?' is no disclaimer of deity but a probe — forcing the man to weigh the title; absolute goodness belongs to God alone (Ellicott, Robertson).
Bede: Christ implies he is God ('none good but God'); Chrysostom: he answered as man one who approached him as a mere teacher, excluding men only from comparison with God's goodness.
Christ points to the second-table commandments (duty to neighbour); 'Defraud not' (for 'covet') is a special probe aimed at the rich man's gains (Ellicott, Barnes).
Bede: the commandments set before him the law's righteousness, the childlike blamelessness proposed for entering the kingdom (under v.17).

The young man confidently claims to have kept all from his youth — sincere and outwardly moral, yet betraying a shallow grasp of the law's spiritual depth (Henry; cf. Romans 7); he drops 'Good' now (Wesley).

Mark alone records Christ's loving look (perhaps the rabbinic kiss of approval, Lightfoot); the command to sell all, give, take up the cross, and follow is a touchstone of whether he loves God above wealth.

Calvin: Christ's 'love' is a real but lesser love for the man's virtues, not saving love (degrees of God's love); perfection lies in keeping the law, which the rich man fails while attached to riches — against the monks' claim of 'perfection.'
Theophylact: a great difference between having and loving riches; Cyril: wealth rightly used is no fault — money may become a door to the kingdom (give to the poor).

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on the stilling of the storm ('What manner of man'), belongs to Mark 4, not this verse.

themes: sell all and give to the poor
take up the cross and follow

schools.summary: His countenance fell (a gloomy, lowering word) and he went away grieved — his sorrow proving his bondage; the riches the world counts a blessing were his ruin (Henry; cf. Matthew 6:24).
Clarke: he might have been a saint and apostle had he been poor — it is oftentimes a misfortune to be rich.

Christ's searching look round the Twelve (peculiar to Mark) draws the lesson from the ruler's departure — 'how hardly shall they that have riches enter,' the difficulty being the hold riches take on the heart (clarified in v.24).
Henry: the rich have temptations the poor escape; those who eagerly seek the world will never prize Christ — watch against the love of riches.

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on baptism by water and the Spirit, is an off-topic catechetical aside, not on this verse.

themes: how hardly shall they that have riches enter
the lesson of the rich ruler

schools.summary: The disciples (counting wealth God's favour) are astonished; Christ tenderly calls them 'Children.' Textual debate: 'that trust in riches' is absent from the best MSS (Ellicott, Robertson omit), but captures his meaning — the peril is trusting, not merely having.
Wesley: he softens the harsh truth by his manner without abating a tittle; hard for a man to have riches and not trust in them.

debates.question: Are the words 'that trust in riches' original to the text?

sides.position: A later gloss, absent from the best MSS, toning down the saying
Capturing Christ's true meaning — the danger is trusting, not having

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on baptism by water and the Spirit, is an off-topic catechetical aside, not on this verse.

schools.summary: The camel and the needle's eye — the largest beast and smallest opening, a deliberate hyperbole for human impossibility; attempts to soften it (a 'gate' or 'cable') miss the point, for the impossibility is the lesson (Henry, Robertson).

outliers.note: The Cyril fragment here, on baptism by water and the Spirit, is an off-topic catechetical aside, not on this verse.

themes: a camel through a needle's eye
the rich man and the kingdom

schools.summary: The disciples, astonished beyond measure, despair — 'Who then can be saved?'; reasoning (from the Jewish view of wealth as God's favour) that if the rich can hardly enter, salvation is hard for all (Henry), driving them from human confidence to God's grace (v.27).

Christ reads their amazement by look and omniscience; Robertson: the impossible with men is possible with God, dismissing the 'needle's-eye gate' theory; Barnes on God's omnipotence (Genesis 18:14; Luke 1:37).
Cyril of Jerusalem: look to God's power, not our weakness, for with God all things are possible — the ground of hope for eternal life.

debates.question: Does the 'needle's eye' refer to a narrow gate, easing the saying?

sides.position: No literal gate — salvation of the rich is humanly impossible, possible only to God

schools.summary: The Gloss: the disciples ask their reward, the rich youth having refused; Bede: leaving all is completed by following Christ — 'we have done what Thou commanded.'
Theophylact: Peter's 'all' was few things, yet held him by affection; he is beatified who leaves even a little that is his all.
Ellicott: Mark omits the thrones-promise (Matthew 19:28), perhaps for Gentile readers; Clarke on the textual 'for the gospel's sake'; the promise embraces every earthly tie.
Theophylact (v.28): the leaving is not neglect of duty but preferring God's glory to the things of this world.

themes: left all for my sake and the gospel's

schools.summary: Barnes: not literal but a hundredfold spiritual compensation (pardon, God's favour, the saints' fellowship); 'with persecutions' (Mark's word) — the reward not exempt from the cross.
Bede records and rejects the millenarian misuse of 'hundredfold now' (the 'Jewish fable' of a thousand years); Theophylact: Peter gained the houses of all the disciples.

outliers.note: Rejects the chiliast reading of 'hundredfold now' as a Jewish fable of a literal earthly restoration.

schools.summary: Robertson: a rebuke to Peter's boast; Ellicott notes Mark omits the vineyard parable Matthew adds to illustrate it.
Theophylact: the Pharisees first became last; the suffering disciples, last here, shall be first by the hope in God.
Bede: the disciples amazed and foreboding; Theophylact: Christ goes before to show he runs to meet his Passion voluntarily, forewarning them item by item lest its suddenness alarm them.
Chrysostom: he joins the resurrection to the passion, that grief might give way to hope; Theophylact: he takes the Twelve apart to reveal the mystery to those closest to him.
The Gloss parses each clause: 'Son of man' suffers (the Godhead cannot), delivered by Judas to the council, condemned, given to Pilate the Gentile.
Cyril: he came to his passion willingly, foretelling its whole course lest the world perish in its sins.
Ellicott: 'spit upon him' shared with Luke not Matthew; Barnes on the prophetic portrait (Psalm 22:6-7); Gill: the Gentile soldiers mock and scourge; the catalogue crowned with the resurrection.
Chrysostom: thinking the kingdom would come before his death, they sought the chief honours, ashamed and apart; Christ questions them to draw out their meaning.
Mark: the brothers ask directly (Matthew via the mother); Robertson: their dream of a Jewish world-empire brushed aside Christ's talk of death.
Gill: the question put to the brothers and the mother together, gathering the whole petition.
Chrysostom (v.35): he asks not from ignorance but to make them answer, exposing their desire to themselves.
Robertson: Mark's 'in thy glory' (Matthew's 'in thy kingdom') — a dream of a Jewish world-empire; Clarke: a kingdom expected on earth; the request for the two chief seats of honour.

themes: sit on thy right and left

schools.summary: Chrysostom: ye speak of honours, I of wrestlings and blood; Cyril: the cup and baptism are martyrdom, which even without water receives the kingdom.
Robertson: Mark has both the cup and baptism (Matthew only the cup), both of death (Mark 14:36; Luke 12:50); Ellicott: the baptism-clause originally Mark's alone.
Wesley: the cup of inward, the baptism of outward sufferings — Christ filled within, covered without.

themes: ye know not what ye ask

schools.summary: Gill: 'We can' betrays double ignorance — of the kingdom and of their own weakness, speaking without dependence on Christ's strength.
Christ turns their boast into prophecy, fulfilled in James's martyrdom (Acts 12:2) and John's sufferings; Chrysostom: ye shall be worthy of martyrdom.
Ellicott: Christ disclaims not the power but the arbitrary giving — the seats go 'to those for whom prepared' by the Father; Chrysostom: he condescends to their meaning.
Wesley: prepared for those who by patient well-doing seek glory; Barnes (Matthew 25:34): the kingdom prepared from the foundation.

debates.question: Does Christ deny the power to give the seats, or only arbitrary giving?

sides.position: Not the power, but the giving by favour — they go to those the Father has prepared

schools.summary: Theophylact: the ten's indignation was envy, the same ambition — undisturbed while each thought himself honoured, displeased only when two reached for the chief places.
Bede: vain both for the two to seek immoderate things and the ten to be annoyed, since greatness comes by humility not power.
Theophylact: Christ calls them near to comfort, then shows that desiring the chief place belongs to the Gentiles, who rule tyrannically as lords.
Ellicott on the two verbs of domineering rule; the Gentile pattern of lordship over subjects, which the disciples were imitating.
Gill: 'so shall it not be among you' — the lordly, heathenish spirit forbidden to the disciples.
Ellicott: 'shall be your minister' (a statement, not 'let him be') — greatness in the kingdom is constituted by service, the way up is down.

themes: so shall it not be among you

schools.summary: Gill: he who desires the highest place and greatest honour must be servant of all.
The intensification — 'chiefest' must be 'servant (doulos) of all,' the higher the place desired the lower the service required.

Robertson on 'ransom for many' (lytron anti pollōn) as Christ's real teaching on his atoning death, preserved by Peter through Mark; Barnes gathers the substitution texts (2 Cor 5:21; Gal 3:13; 1 Tim 2:6).
Bede: the Son of man's service the crowning example; 'for many, not all,' i.e. for those who would believe; Theophylact: what greater than to die for those he serves?
Wesley: 'a ransom for many' = for as many souls as needed such a ransom (2 Cor 5:15).

debates.question: For whom did Christ give his life a 'ransom'?

sides.position: For as many as need the ransom

schools.summary: Bede harmonises the Gospels (two blind men / one, entering / leaving) as fuller and briefer reports, not contradictions; Mark names the more notable; allegory of Jericho ('moon'), the gospel to Jews then Gentiles; Jerome on Jericho as anathema.

debates.question: How are the Gospels reconciled — two blind men or one, entering or leaving Jericho?

sides.position: Not contradiction but fuller and briefer reports of the same event; Mark names the more notable

outliers.note: Reads the number (one healed leaving, two entering in Matthew) allegorically of the gospel going first to the Jews, then to Jews and Gentiles.

themes: the last healing before the Passion

schools.summary: Gill: he learned it was Jesus by the crowd's noise; 'Son of David' confesses Jesus as Messiah — the blind man seeing by faith what the seeing crowd did not.

Robertson on the tenses (kept rebuking; he cried the more); Gill: they would silence his 'Son of David'; Barnes on seeking with the whole heart (Jeremiah 29:13) — faith persevering against discouragement.

Robertson: Jesus stood still (halting his journey for one beggar), 'he calleth thee' joyful news, vivid presents; Ellicott: 'Call him' the better text, the bystanders' 'Be of good comfort' Mark's alone.
Ellicott on the vivid casting-off and leaping up; Barnes and Clarke draw the application — cast away the garments of self-righteousness and run to Christ; Clarke's anecdote of the converted negro.

outliers.note: Tells the anecdote of the converted negro who found salvation quickly because, having little 'clothing,' he could throw it off and run to Christ.

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'Rabboni' (as John 20:16), Mark reproducing the very syllable; Robertson: the Messiah expected to give sight (Isaiah 61:1); the same question as to James and John, but a humble answer — only sight.

themes: that I might receive my sight

schools.summary: Barnes: 'made whole' or 'saved thee,' joining bodily and spiritual; Ellicott: Bartimaeus went up with the company, named because he became a known eyewitness in the Church.
Clarke: a proof of Christ's sovereign power used only in benevolence — how slow to punish, how prone to spare.

themes: thy faith hath made thee whole
